figures.caption: Table 1: Results on DomainAuth (fine-grained, 10-level) reported as Spearman’s ρ (%) and Kendall’s τ (%). Bold: best; underline: second best, per LLM.

Figure 5: An example from the RAGAuth dataset. Each instance includes a yes/no question, the ground-truth answer, and a list of 10 retrieved documents with their source URL, domain, PageRank score, and text snippet. The task is to generate a correct answer based on this information.

Figure 1: An illustration of the authority perception challenge in RAG. When faced with conflicting information from sources of varying authority (e.g., a high-authority medical institution like Mayo Clinic vs. lower-authority lifestyle blogs), an LLM must correctly discern which source to trust to provide a reliable answer. Our work investigates this capability.

Figure 4: Authority-aware RAG pipeline: retrieved documents are LLM-scored; top-k go to the generator. Filtering signals: (a) w/o Filter; (b) Relevance Filter; (c) Utility Filter; (d) Authority Filter (source URL only; no document content). All prompts in this section are provided in Appendix A.
Table 2: Results on DomainAuth (coarse-grained, 5-level) reported as Spearman’s ρ (%) and Kendall’s τ (%). Bold: best; underline: second best, per model.
Table 4: Results on EntityAuth (fine-grained, 10-level, w/o text) across three domains reported as Spearman’s ρ (%) and Kendall’s τ (%). Bold: best; underline: second best, per LLM per domain.
Table 5: Answer accuracy (%) on RAGAuth across four models under different filtering strategies and topk settings (k ∈ {1, 3, 5}). Bold: best per LLM per k.

Figure 2: An overview of AuthorityBench. It consists of three sub-tasks: DomainAuth for source authority, EntityAuth for entity authority, and RAGAuth for downstream RAG evaluation. The outer rings show the topic distribution within each dataset.
Table 9: PairJudge ranking derivation methods on DomainAuth (fine-grained). Spearman’s ρ (%). Bold: best; underline: second best, per row.
Table 10: PairJudge ranking derivation methods on DomainAuth (coarse-grained). Spearman’s ρ (%). Bold: best; underline: second best, per row.

Figure 6: Heatmap of prediction bias (predicted - true score) on DomainAuth for the Pointwise-Judge. Red cells indicate overestimation, while blue cells indicate underestimation. Most models tend to overestimate low-authority sources and underestimate high-authority ones.

Table 7: Overview of the AuthorityBench. Corpus is the number of unique items. †Listwise instances are split between N=5 and N=10 formats. ‡Pairwise instances are split between hard (0 < ∆PageRank≤2) and easy (∆PageRank≥5) pairs. EntityAuth uses only N=10 lists and does not include a Pairwise setting. For RAGAuth, each query is paired with 10 retrieved webpage documents.
Table 6: Results on EntityAuth (coarse-grained, 5-level, w/o text) across three domains reported as Spearman’s ρ and Kendall’s τ (%). Bold: best; underline: second best, per model per domain.
